items.prompt: Why should an operation budget be stated explicitly instead of using a universal rule such as "10^8 operations always fit"?

options.text: Actual throughput depends on operation cost, language, hardware, memory behavior, and time limit.
Big-O already gives an exact runtime in seconds.
Variable names determine the machine speed.
Constraints never influence algorithm choice.

feedback.reason: A rough budget is a modeling assumption, not a universal physical constant.
feedback.details: Content should state the abstract budget and what counts as one unit operation before comparing candidate algorithms.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.big_o_exact: Asymptotic notation omits constants and hardware.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.budget_unneeded: Input limits are a primary design signal.

items.prompt: With n = 200,000 and an abstract budget of 100,000,000 unit operations, which candidate is clearly infeasible by its dominant count?

options.text: An O(n²) full pair comparison.
An O(n) scan.
An O(n log2 n) comparison process.
An O(1) check.

feedback.reason: n² is approximately 40 billion when n = 200,000.
feedback.details: That exceeds the stated 100 million unit-operation budget by orders of magnitude.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.linear: About 200,000 unit operations is well below the budget.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.n_log_n: It is roughly a few million operations under the stated abstraction.

constraints: Use an abstract budget of 100,000,000 equal-cost unit operations and ignore constant factors.

items.prompt: With n = 20, what does the size of the full subset space suggest?

options.text: 2^20 is about one million, so complete enumeration may be viable under a suitable per-state cost and budget.
Any exponential algorithm is automatically impossible.
2^20 grows linearly with n.
There are only 20 subsets.

feedback.reason: Asymptotic class alone is not the whole feasibility decision.
feedback.details: Small n can make an exponential state space manageable, but per-state work and output obligations still matter.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.twenty_states: Each of 20 elements has an include/exclude choice.

items.prompt: With n = 50, why is enumerating all 2^n subsets normally rejected under a 100,000,000-operation budget?

options.text: 2^50 is over one quadrillion states, far beyond the budget.
The code would probably use nested loops.
Subset enumeration produces no output.
Every algorithm with n = 50 is infeasible.

feedback.reason: The candidate state count itself exceeds the budget by many orders of magnitude.
feedback.details: No constant-factor optimization can bridge that gap under the stated model.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.nested_name: Syntax is not the decisive argument.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.fifty_large: Polynomial algorithms can be easy at n = 50.

constraints: Use an abstract budget of 100,000,000 equal-cost unit operations.

items.prompt: An algorithm preprocesses n records in O(n log n) and answers q queries in O(log n) each. Which workload expression should be compared with the budget?

options.text: O(n log n + q log n).
O(log n) only.
O(n log n) only.
O(nq log² n).

feedback.reason: Feasibility is based on the full requested workload.
feedback.details: A fast per-query bound does not erase one-time preprocessing or the number of queries.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.query_only: This ignores both preprocessing and query count.

items.prompt: Two input dimensions are n = 10,000 and m = 10,000. What should be checked before accepting an O(nm) nested comparison?

options.text: Compute nm = 100,000,000 and compare it with the stated budget and per-comparison cost.
Rename m to n so the loop looks quadratic.
Use n + m because there are two inputs.
Only the larger dimension matters.

feedback.reason: Multidimensional constraints should be substituted into the actual cost expression.
feedback.details: The same O(nm) class can be feasible or infeasible depending on the separate limits and constant work.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.add_only: The algorithm compares every pair, so counts multiply.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ignore_m: Both dimensions participate in the product.

items.prompt: Why can two algorithms with the same Big-O classification differ in feasibility near a tight limit?

options.text: Their constant factors, operation types, memory access, and implementation overhead can differ.
Big-O is never useful for comparing algorithms.
Their variable names may be longer.
Algorithms in the same class always have identical runtime.

feedback.reason: Asymptotic filtering rejects clearly wrong growth classes first.
feedback.details: Near the budget boundary, the cost model and implementation details need closer measurement or conservative margin.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.big_o_wrong: It remains essential for growth but is not an exact stopwatch.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_always: Asymptotic equivalence hides constants and lower-order terms.

items.prompt: A task must output k records. Why must k be considered when assessing feasibility?

options.text: Writing or materializing k records requires at least proportional work and usually proportional output storage.
Output is free once the algorithm computes the first record.
Only input size can affect complexity.
k is always constant.

feedback.reason: A candidate time bound smaller than the mandatory output size is incomplete.
feedback.details: Feasibility must include both search/computation and required emission.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.output_free: Every required record must still be produced.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.k_constant: The result count may grow with the input.

items.prompt: A proposed O(n²) dynamic-programming table uses n = 50,000 boolean entries per dimension. Which resource issue appears before runtime details?

options.text: The table has 2.5 billion cells and may exceed the memory budget.
Booleans always occupy zero memory asymptotically.
Only CPU time matters when evaluating constraints.
The table is large only because both dimensions are named n.

feedback.reason: Constraint translation includes memory as well as operations.
feedback.details: A theoretically acceptable transition count is irrelevant if the state representation cannot fit.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.time_only: Memory can reject an approach independently.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.names_quadratic: The actual cell product causes the growth.

items.prompt: Which quantities should be included when translating constraints into a budget? Select all that apply.

options.text: Maximum values of every independent input dimension.
The number of repeated operations or queries.
The maximum required output volume.
The spelling of loop variables.

feedback.reason: Feasibility follows the complete workload shape.
feedback.details: Ignoring one dimension, query multiplicity, or output can understate cost by orders of magnitude.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.variable_names: Names do not affect operation count.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.output_size: Output creates an unavoidable lower bound.

items.prompt: Under an abstract 100,000,000-operation budget and n = 100,000, which candidate classes are plausibly viable before constant-factor review? Select all that apply.

options.text: O(n).
O(n log2 n).
O(n²).
O(n³).

feedback.reason: Substituting the maximum n gives a first-pass feasibility filter.
feedback.details: The accepted classes still require realistic operation and memory modeling.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.quadratic: About 10 billion units, which exceeds the budget.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.cubic: About 10^15 units, which is far beyond the budget.

constraints: Treat one dominant-count unit as one abstract operation and ignore constant factors.

items.prompt: Which exhaustive spaces can be plausible under a 100,000,000-state budget? Select all that apply.

options.text: All 2^20 subsets.
All 2^26 subsets.
All 2^40 subsets.
All 12! permutations.

feedback.reason: Concrete state counts matter when n is small.
feedback.details: Accepted cases remain only first-pass plausible; output and per-state work can still make them impractical.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.subsets_26: This is about 67 million states before per-state overhead.

constraints: Use an abstract budget of 100,000,000 states before per-state work.

items.prompt: Which caveats belong in an operation-budget estimate? Select all that apply.

options.text: The estimate treats operations as abstract equal-cost units.
Constant factors and cache behavior may matter near the limit.
Memory and output constraints may reject an otherwise acceptable operation count.
The estimate proves every implementation finishes in the same wall-clock time.

feedback.reason: A good estimate states what it includes and what remains uncertain.
feedback.details: The budget is a design tool, not a universal hardware law.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.universal_speed: No abstract budget gives that guarantee.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.constants: Asymptotics are a filter, not an exact timing result.

items.prompt: Arrange the process for assessing an algorithm against constraints.

elements.text: Extract maximum input dimensions, query counts, output obligations, and memory limits.
Write the candidate algorithm's time and space expressions.
Substitute the maximum constraints to estimate work and storage.
Reject, accept provisionally, or benchmark when the estimate is near the budget.

feedback.reason: Constraint analysis connects asymptotic formulas to concrete maxima.
feedback.details: Near-boundary cases require conservative constants or measurement rather than false certainty.

items.prompt: Arrange the analysis of a repeated-query workload.

elements.text: Compare P(n) + q·Q(n), memory, and update costs with the workload budget.

feedback.reason: Fast query time is meaningful only inside the total workload.
feedback.details: The same data structure can be attractive for many queries and wasteful for one.

items.prompt: Arrange the analysis of a two-dimensional candidate cost O(nm).

elements.text: Keep n and m as separate constrained dimensions.
Estimate the product and per-pair work.
Compare the result with time and memory budgets.

feedback.reason: Collapsing dimensions too early can hide the real maximum product.
feedback.details: The separate limits may be asymmetric and should be preserved.

items.prompt: For n = 200,000, estimate the dominant operation count of O(n log2 n) and whether it fits an abstract 100,000,000-operation budget.
constraints: Use log2(200,000) ≈ 18 and ignore constant factors.

feedback.reason: Multiplying 200,000 by about 18 gives roughly 3.6 million dominant units.
feedback.details: That is below the stated abstract budget, before constant-factor review.

items.prompt: For n = 30,000, estimate the dominant count of an O(n²) algorithm and whether it fits an abstract 100,000,000-operation budget.

feedback.details: The dominant count exceeds the budget ninefold before overhead.

items.prompt: For n = 25, estimate the number of subset masks 2^n and whether one O(1)-work pass over all masks fits an abstract 100,000,000-operation budget.

feedback.reason: The mask count is about 33.6 million.
feedback.details: It fits the abstract state budget, but materializing subsets or doing O(n) work per mask could change the decision.

items.prompt: A workload has n = 100,000 records and q = 100,000 queries. Preprocessing costs O(n), and every query costs O(log2 n). Estimate dominant units and budget fit for a 100,000,000-operation budget.
constraints: Use log2(100,000) ≈ 17 and ignore constant factors.

feedback.reason: The estimate is 100,000 + 100,000·17 ≈ 1.8 million.
feedback.details: The per-query logarithm must be multiplied by q before comparing with the budget.
